steps.title: Keep the action in the past
steps.content: The sentence describes a historical event: after winning the 1860 presidential election, Lincoln appointed several former rivals to his cabinet. The later clause explains why historians praise that decision. Because the appointment and use of those rivals' talents happened in the past, the blank needs a simple past-tense verb.

steps.title: Reject the nonpast tenses
steps.content:
• Choice A - 'has leveraged': The present perfect suggests a past action connected to the present, which does not fit this completed historical action.
  • Choice B - 'is leveraging': The present progressive suggests ongoing action now, but Lincoln's cabinet decision occurred long ago.
  • Choice C - 'will leverage': The future tense points to an action that has not happened yet, which conflicts with the historical context.
The remaining choice uses simple past tense and matches the completed action described in the sentence. The answer is D.
